Read the spec. Workout.md is a minimal-friction workout tracker: tracking-first with rich structured inputs, Markdown as the storage layer, AI as a planning layer (not a chatbot), deviations as first-class data, plan repair forward (no streaks/guilt), and configurable coach policy. Asked what the user wants to do next.
how do you imagine the app? high level

Described a high-level vision: one main "Today" screen you live in during workouts, a lightweight plan/calendar timeline, and a coach/context settings area. AI is invisible by default — surfacing as ghost targets, inline nudges, and a single "what should I do?" repair action. Core loop is open → log sets fast → deviations captured normally → coach quietly repairs tomorrow. Markdown is the substrate, never the UI; no streaks or shame.
make a wireframe runnable prototype use the multiple skills I added for you: ios-hig-design, mobile-ios-design, liquid-glass-design run it on my computere asap use sonnet agents to do the coding
Noted that only `liquid-glass-design` is installed; the other two skill names weren't found, so iOS HIG principles will be applied directly. Will load the skill and hand the build to a Sonnet agent.

Built and opened a self-contained single-file HTML prototype in the browser with CSS Liquid Glass styling — three working tabs (Today, Plan, Coach) with ghosted prescribed vs. solid actual sets, coach nudges as diffs, neutral missed-day styling, and a working Markdown export button. Noted this is a web emulation of Liquid Glass, not the native iOS 26 material.

no, build it as an actual ios app -- I have xcode and everything! don't use card containers -- those are for the web and look bad on iphone -- bleed-edge instead. I do circuits or supersets very often so make that representable on the UI. the UI should be tik-tok like where I only see what set I need to do next and perhaps cues or something that can be attached by the coach for the exercise. I should be able to tap next or provide very easily feedback on how easy/hard it felt or write a note or whatever. open it on my ios simulator when ready
Launched the native iOS app and started capturing screenshots to verify rendering.

Confirmed the runner screen looks correct — full-bleed, floating glass top strip, giant hero target, coach cue pill, effort pills, reps stepper, and glass control cluster. Advancing into a superset block to verify the circuit mini-map.
Confirmed the superset page renders with round indicator, mini-map, purple block hue, and coach cue. Caught a mid-scroll frame; taking a clean settled screenshot.
Applied a fix for page ghosting caused by safe area insets shrinking the scroll container. Rebuilding and reinstalling.
Confirmed ghosting is resolved — clean single full-screen pages with opaque edge-to-edge backgrounds. Verifying a superset page is equally clean.

App is running clean on the simulator. Summarized the final product: a native SwiftUI iOS 26 app with real Liquid Glass APIs, TikTok-style one-set-per-page runner, full-bleed no-cards design, first-class superset/circuit support with mini-maps and block hue shifts, coach cues per exercise, quick effort/note/skip feedback, and full-height page snapping. Noted minor control spacing and unfinished Done/circuit screenshots, and offered next steps.
